message.content: Adversaries can exploit stolen web session cookies to authenticate to web applications and services, effectively bypassing multi-factor authentication (MFA) protocols. This technique leverages the fact that session cookies, which are used to maintain authenticated sessions without repeatedly passing credentials, can remain valid for extended periods. Once an adversary obtains a session cookie, they can import it into a browser they control, gaining access to the web application as the authenticated user. This access allows them to read emails, access sensitive information, and perform actions permitted by the victim's account. Common methods of obtaining these cookies include malware designed to steal session cookies. Notable examples include APT29 using stolen cookies to access cloud resources and bypass MFA. Mitigation strategies include implementing short session durations, regularly invalidating session cookies, employing robust endpoint security to detect and prevent cookie theft, and using additional layers of authentication beyond cookies.
